name: Louis Armstrong
description: American jazz musician, trumpeter and singer (1901–1971)
abstract: Louis Daniel Armstrong (August 4, 1901 – July 6, 1971), nicknamed "Satchmo", "Satch", and "Pops", was an American jazz and blues trumpeter and vocalist. Among the most influential figures in jazz, his career spanned five decades and several eras in the history of the genre.

quotes.text: walk out on the stage anyways I always read about Jerusalem in the Bible never had no idea the minute we walk out on that stage you'd hear the same thing you'd hear at Carnegie Hall Sancho yeah man you know what I mean well you know the show's on yeah I mean you can tell yeah you plead all over the same that's why I say a note to note in any language and no problem to go over there there people and worry about nothing
quotes.caption: General comparison of stage atmosphere to Carnegie Hall

quotes.text: Even when I was in school, I played all classical music. When I was in the orphanage, we played duets. And that was the music. So that instills the soul in you. You know? Litz, Bach, Bach, Marnonoff, Gustav Mahler, and Haydn. We had a little laugh there, where he kept on saying, Haydn, Haydn. And I said, well, let him come out. You know? That was Haydn's style, too. He'd surprise you any time. Every composer that there was, we used it in this song. I'm glad you mentioned Mahler. He's one of my favorite composers. Yeah. And that's it. That's it, when he said, Gustav Mahler, I said, well, don't forget, Spatz Mahler. Well, he was a composer, too, but later music, you know? I think, chronologically, if those greats, the great symphonic composers, had been around today, I think they would have adapted themselves to our music and been just as great. I mean, Well, that's what the musicians showed appreciation by playing their music in swing time. That's why I say the foreign arrangers did so much for America because they brought all that, because that's all they played. And look what the Three Penny Opera did for Mac the Knife. You understand? Yes, of course. Probably they all played classical music for years, and that's where they read, them musicians, read and everything. I mean, no insinuation or anything like that, but you wonder, are they good musicians? Well, they're good musicians in front. See? Now, they come to America and they say, well, look at you. That's the one that's the little beat in it. Yes. Probably wouldn't bear it in their mind. And they're the one that brought the idea to us, which I'm very grateful and I meet them all over the world. See? So we all have something in common to thank each other, appreciate each other, every musician you meet. There's a lot of English bands. That boy came over here and his ovation was so big at the Carnegie Hall, they had to put the lights out. He had took a hundred boughs. So he had to put the lights out and he's from England. Tramon player. He played all kind of classics. And I think you got to be a good musician in the heart. You know. You got to have that equipment there to call on it. classical background of someone. See? And them brass bands was a lot of experience for me. Then comes into this symphony orchestra and I'm playing at a nightclub and they only hired me to be the hot man in the band. Because when the lights goes up, they play an overture. You know. And there was days where when they play a tune like Spanish Shaw or something like that, well that's where I come in. But still in all, I got to work when the lights go out. So when you look around, I'm sitting there playing second trumpet one week. And first trumpet one week. And your environment makes you. You got to improve if you enjoy or want to get someplace in there and you enjoy what you're doing. How can I miss? All they have to worry about the different divisions and the classical music. You know how those different. Well I'm sitting there getting a million dollar experience and they getting the experience from the beat. that ain't still in there. Fifteen pieces. What do you think all these things that are happening like rock and roll and bop and way out and that stuff, where are we going to be? Are we going to be around forever? Just keep blowing your horn, perhaps. Because probably just like anything else they'll fall for a fad. Fads just last so long. You haven't heard the word bop until I just mentioned it. That's for years. Yeah. It's just like I remember one time I was a kid and there's a fellow at the gate telling, right this way folks see the horse's head where his tail ought to be. See, everybody is climbing to pay this dime and get in there the guy that's got a horse turned around. Well that one time he got away with it. That's the same as all that jujitsu music. You know but they can't make a rebound and they can't return trip like we were. We go to the same place four or five times a year and you have the same audience. Same as Heifetz, Marion Anderson. You don't need 9,000 weeks advertising. You don't have to build up because the people who get the tickets they ain't saying a word. But we look around and there's the audience right there. We played Carnegie Hall night after Christmas. See? All the stars will be jizz. You know what I mean? So it's just another date when I walk out on that stage. I play the same thing that I played down in the arena in the same way. I'd stand on your head even. Then we all agree that it's here to stay. Cut and stay. Keep it swinging. Don't worry about it. Bob Hacken's doing all right. Sure is. Jack's doing all right but he's bad. I'm sure that Bunny and Bix are doing all right too.
quotes.caption: Recalling an English clarinetist's ovation at Carnegie Hall

about_quotes.speaker: Freddie Moore
about_quotes.evidence: The transcript discusses 'Pops Foster' (Edmond Hall's bassist, often called Pops Foster, distinct from Louis Armstrong who is also sometimes called Pops, but the context clearly refers to the bassist Pops Foster). The name 'Louis Armstrong' does not appear in the text. The interviewer asks about 'Po

about_quotes.speaker: Dawoud Sabu Adeyola
about_quotes.evidence: There’s a TV on you know, there’s Louis Armstrong. Louis Armstrong man, with his trumpet in one hand, a hankie in the other hand. And he’s clean, he’s got a tuxedo on. She said, “He’s the best in the world. There you go. He’s the best.”
about_quotes.doc_title: Dawoud Sabu Adeyola interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, January 30, 2025

about_quotes.speaker: Allan Vaché
about_quotes.evidence: There was a lot of great music being made. There was Louis Armstrong... I mean if you want to hear something innovative put on Louis’ duet record with Earl Hines doing “Weatherbird Rag.” It’s the most amazing thing I’ve ever heard.
about_quotes.doc_title: Allan Vaché interviewed by Monk Rowe, Scottsdale, Arizona, April 15, 2000

about_quotes.speaker: Sonny Igoe
about_quotes.evidence: The transcript mentions 'Pops' as a nickname used by Benny Goodman for the interviewee ('So Benny looked up like this. And he said, “stay up there, Pops.”'). There is no mention of Louis Armstrong in the text.
about_quotes.doc_title: Sonny Igoe interviewed by Monk Rowe, Emerson, New Jersey, July 31, 2003

about_quotes.speaker: Bob Wilber
about_quotes.evidence: My discovery of earlier jazz really was because the big companies, Columbia and Victor, started re-issuing the old records of Louis Armstrong... I remember listening to the re-issue of Armstrong’s “Hot Five.”
about_quotes.doc_title: Bob Wilber interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, May 22, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Alan Broadbent
about_quotes.evidence: the man that invented it was Louis Armstrong of course... I listen to all kinds of music... with Bud Powell or Bird or Lester Young or Louis Armstrong... You can really feel how they push and pull the time
about_quotes.doc_title: Alan Broadbent interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, October 26, 2023
